tier_intro: This is where your AI agent gets a personality. Not a generic chatbot — your brand's voice. The difference between 'Your request has been received' and 'Got it! We're on it.' Takes 5–7 minutes.

tier_complete.headline: Brand voice calibrated.
tier_complete.body: Your AI agent now has a distinct personality that matches how your team actually communicates. Customers will feel the difference — it sounds like you, not a script. Next: tell it what you sell.

questions.question: If your brand was a person at a dinner party, they'd be...

options.label: The Professor — knowledgeable, thorough, loves to explain things properly
options.example: "Let me walk you through exactly how this works..."

options.label: The Friend — warm, casual, makes everything feel easy
options.example: "Hey! Don't worry, this happens all the time — here's what we'll do..."

options.label: The Expert — confident, direct, gets to the point fast
The Entertainer — fun, memorable, leaves you smiling
options.example: "Great news and slightly less great news — which do you want first?"

options.label: The Advisor — calm, measured, helps you think through the decision
options.example: "Before I answer, let me make sure I understand what you need..."

questions.meta_profiling: Archetype chosen is the single most important brand voice signal. Reveals how the company sees its relationship with customers.
questions.why_this_matters: This one choice shapes every single response the AI generates — word choice, sentence length, use of humor, how it handles complaints. It's the personality core.

questions.question: Customer emails from your team sound like...

options.label: Very formal — 'Dear Mr. Smith, I am writing to inform you...'
Professional but warm — 'Hi John, Thanks for reaching out...'
Casual and conversational — 'Hey! Saw your message...'
options.example: Like texting a colleague you know well

options.label: Ultra casual / playful — 'Yooo! We got you 🙌'

questions.meta_profiling: Formality level is the second most important dimension. Calibrates vocabulary complexity, sentence structure, and acceptable abbreviations.
questions.why_this_matters: Formality mismatch kills trust. A premium law firm's AI shouldn't say 'yooo'. A youth brand's AI shouldn't say 'I am pleased to inform you'. This is the calibration.

questions.question: Is humor OK in customer interactions?

options.label: Never — we're professional, humor risks coming across wrong
Light touches — a friendly joke when the moment is right, nothing edgy
Core of brand — humor is how we stand out, lean into it

questions.meta_profiling: Humor policy reveals brand confidence and risk tolerance. 'Never' often signals conservative industry or past brand incidents.
questions.why_this_matters: AI humor without a policy becomes unpredictable. This setting prevents the AI from cracking a joke when a customer is upset — or being robotically serious when your brand is playful.

questions.question: Which personality traits best describe your brand? Pick up to 5.

options.label: Trustworthy
Innovative
Bold
Caring
Premium
Approachable
Straightforward
Empathetic
Energetic
Calm
Witty
Authoritative
Humble
Inspirational
Practical
Sophisticated
Playful
Transparent
Efficient
Passionate

questions.meta_profiling: Which traits they DON'T pick is as important as what they pick. The gap between selected traits and their formality level reveals brand tension worth flagging.
questions.why_this_matters: These traits are injected into AI's response generation. 'Straightforward + Empathetic' creates very different responses than 'Sophisticated + Authoritative'.

questions.question: Are there words or phrases your team should NEVER use?
questions.placeholder: e.g. 'cheap', 'unfortunately', 'I'm just a bot', 'I cannot assist', competitor names, industry jargon you hate...
questions.meta_profiling: Forbidden words reveal past brand incidents, competitive sensitivities, or strong founder opinions. Each one is a story.
questions.why_this_matters: One wrong word can undo a customer interaction. 'Unfortunately' signals failure before explaining anything. 'Cheap' undercuts your value. AI hard-blocks these by default.

questions.question: Are there phrases your team ALWAYS uses — signature language, sign-offs, greetings?
questions.placeholder: e.g. 'Happy to help!', 'Let's make it happen.', 'Warm regards, the [Company] team', specific taglines...
questions.meta_profiling: Signature phrases reveal brand investment in consistency. Detailed answer = strong brand culture. Blank = early stage or informal team.
questions.why_this_matters: AI uses these consistently across every interaction — reinforcing brand recognition in every touchpoint.

questions.question: How technical should customer communication be?

options.label: Zero jargon — explain everything like the customer is smart but not an expert
Some industry terms are fine — customers know the basics
Technical is fine — our customers are experts who prefer precision
Depends on the customer — AI should detect and adapt

questions.meta_profiling: Technical level reveals assumed customer sophistication. 'Depends on context' = mature brand that understands customer segmentation.
questions.why_this_matters: Over-explaining to experts is condescending. Under-explaining to novices loses them. This calibrates the default register.

questions.question: What's your emoji policy in customer messages?

options.label: Never — we keep it clean text
Occasionally — one emoji max, only when it adds warmth (✓ ✉️ 👋)
Regularly — we use them to add energy and expression 🎉
We have specific emojis that fit our brand (I'll list them)

questions.meta_profiling: Emoji use reveals platform context (email vs. chat), brand age (older brands = no emoji), and audience demographics.
questions.why_this_matters: Emoji in the wrong context looks unprofessional. Absence in the right context feels cold. This single setting transforms message tone.

questions.question: When speaking as your company, do you use 'I', 'we', or your company name?

options.label: 'We' — we're a team, not a person ("We'll look into this")
'I' — feels more personal and direct ("I'll look into this")
Company name — formal, third-person brand voice ("[Company] will look into this")
Mixed — 'I' for personal moments, 'we' for company actions

questions.meta_profiling: Person/pronoun reveals whether AI is representing a brand or a person. 'I' = intimate, personal. 'We' = corporate. 'Company name' = formal/legal.
questions.why_this_matters: This shapes every single sentence AI generates. Getting it wrong creates a jarring disconnect — like a bank that suddenly says 'I totally get you!'

questions.question: How should AI greet customers at the start of a conversation?

options.label: Personal and warm — 'Hi [Name]! How can I help?'
Professional — 'Hello [Name], how can I assist you today?'
Branded — 'Welcome to [Company]! What can we do for you?'
Direct — skip pleasantries, go straight to helping
I have a specific greeting I want to use

questions.meta_profiling: 'Direct' choice often correlates with technical audiences or high-volume support contexts where speed is valued over warmth.
questions.why_this_matters: First impression sets the emotional tone for the entire interaction. Customers decide in the first sentence whether this feels like a good experience.

questions.question: How should conversations end?

options.label: Warm close — 'Is there anything else I can help with today?'
Satisfaction check — 'Did that solve your problem?'
Invite back — 'Don't hesitate to reach out anytime!'
Formal — 'Thank you for contacting [Company]. Have a good day.'
Quick feedback — end with a 1-5 rating request
No forced close — let the conversation end naturally

questions.meta_profiling: Closing style reveals what the company prioritizes — customer satisfaction, efficiency, feedback, or relationship-building.
questions.why_this_matters: The close determines whether customers leave feeling helped or processed. It also affects whether they come back.

questions.question: When something goes wrong, your brand's response sounds like...

options.label: Acknowledge first — 'I completely understand your frustration, and I'm sorry this happened.'
Fix first — 'Let's fix this immediately. Here's what we're going to do...'
Investigate first — 'Let me look into this right now and get back to you with exactly what happened.'
Empathy + action together — acknowledge and offer solution in the same breath

questions.meta_profiling: Apology style reveals company culture around accountability. 'Fix first' = action-oriented. 'Acknowledge first' = emotionally intelligent. Both are valid.
questions.why_this_matters: Complaints handled well turn frustrated customers into loyal ones. The wrong apology style amplifies the problem. This is one of the highest-impact AI settings.

questions.question: What's your brand's energy level?

options.label: Calm and measured — steady, never rushed, composed
Warm and steady — friendly without being excitable
Upbeat and positive — enthusiastic, encouraging, glass half full
High energy — exciting, dynamic, lots of forward momentum

questions.meta_profiling: Energy level often correlates with brand archetype — high energy with The Entertainer, calm with The Advisor. Mismatches are worth noting.
questions.why_this_matters: Energy bleeds into punctuation, word choice, and response length. A calm brand never uses '!!!'. A high-energy brand never sends two-word responses.

questions.question: Do you serve customers from different cultural backgrounds where language sensitivity matters?

options.label: Mostly one culture — we can calibrate for our primary audience
Some diversity — AI should avoid idioms that don't travel well
Highly diverse — AI must be culturally neutral, avoid all region-specific references
Multilingual required — customers expect responses in their language

questions.meta_profiling: Diversity level triggers language safeguards — prevents AI from using sports metaphors, religious references, or idioms that confuse international customers.
questions.why_this_matters: AI that says 'ballpark figure' to a non-English speaker, or makes a cultural assumption, loses trust immediately. This calibrates for safe, global communication.

questions.question: Do you have an existing brand guidelines document you'd like to upload?
questions.placeholder: Upload brand guidelines, tone of voice doc, or style guide
questions.meta_profiling: Existence of formal brand guidelines reveals marketing maturity and brand investment.
questions.why_this_matters: A brand guidelines doc can override or enrich everything in this tier — the AI will follow your existing standards, not generic best practices.
